nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, reef crests, and mangrove-adjacent areas; most commonly found between 5 and 20 m depth, with records to approximately 40 m

diet.notes: Pocillopora derives most of its energy from zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae living within its tissue) that photosynthesize under aquarium lighting. Supplemental feeding with micro-plankton, rotifers, or comparable fine-particulate foods approximately twice per week has been documented to support growth and coloration. Feeding is most effective when offered via pipette or broadcast into the water column during periods of polyp extension. Calcium, strontium, and trace elements should be dosed consistently to support skeletal growth.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS and soft corals with spacing
avoidWith: corals placed in direct physical contact
aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x900. Pocillopora verrucosa, Réunion. Tack-sharp in-situ colony, vibrant pink-red with clearly visible verrucae; classic genus look.
Ahmed Abdul Rahman; 4000x3000. Pocillopora damicornis, Maldives. Sharp in-situ pink cauliflower colony, whole colony framed, delicate branching.
Brocken Inaglory; 2000x1500. Pocillopora meandrina with resident fish. Sharp, vibrant pink colony showing meandrina branch form.
Charles J. Sharp; 6982x4658. Pocillopora grandis (antler coral) with blue damselfish, Moorea. High-res, sharp reef scene; distinct species with different growth form.
Rosie Steinberg. Pocillopora damicornis, deep maroon-red morph, sharp in-situ close colony; adds color variety.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x848. Pocillopora meandrina, Réunion. Sharp magenta-pink colony, whole colony in frame.
Diego Delso; 5708x4394. Pocillopora verrucosa, Red Sea. Very high-res, sharp, detailed verrucae on pinkish colony.
U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service (Pacific Region). Pocillopora meandrina, sharp pink-tipped in-situ colony.

summary: Pocillopora Coral is widely regarded as one of the most beginner-accessible SPS (small polyp stony) corals in the reef hobby — generally more forgiving than Acropora, fast-growing, and broadly available as captive-bred frags in green, pink, purple, and designer color morphs.
careNotes:
Pocillopora is a genus of approximately 20 or more described species, most of which share very similar husbandry requirements and are commonly discussed at the genus level in the aquarium trade. Pocillopora damicornis (the Cauliflower Coral) is the most iconic and widely kept species, though P. verrucosa, P. meandrina, and captive-bred designer morphs such as the WWC Rainbow Pocillopora are also frequently encountered.

Pocillopora is widely regarded as one of the most beginner-accessible SPS (small polyp stony) corals in the reef hobby. Unlike Acropora, established colonies have been reported to tolerate moderate parameter swings and less-than-perfect water chemistry, making them a frequently recommended starting point for hobbyists transitioning into SPS corals for the first time.

Lighting: Moderate to high light intensity is generally advised, targeting approximately 100–300 PAR (photosynthetically active radiation, a measure of light energy available for photosynthesis). New frags should be introduced lower in the rockwork and moved gradually upward over 2–4 weeks to allow them to acclimate without bleaching. Pocillopora tends to tolerate a wider range of light intensities than Acropora.

Flow: High, chaotic (non-directed) water movement is advised. Strong, turbulent flow helps prevent detritus from settling into the colony's branching structure and reduces susceptibility to bacterial infection. Maintaining stable alkalinity (8–12 dKH) is important; a two-part dosing system or calcium reactor is commonly used to achieve this.

Growth rate: Pocillopora is generally considered a rapid grower among SPS corals. Colonies can expand quickly and encroach on neighboring corals, so planning adequate spacing — at least 5–8 cm from neighboring colonies — is recommended from the start.

Pest and disease susceptibility: New frags should be dipped in a coral-safe dip solution before introduction to the display tank. Pocillopora is susceptible to rapid tissue necrosis (RTN) — a fast-spreading bacterial infection that can consume tissue within hours — and other bacterial infections under stress. The genus has shown some documented resistance to bleaching relative to other SPS, though temperatures above 28–29°C can still trigger bleaching events.

Aggression: Pocillopora tends to be relatively non-aggressive compared to other SPS corals. Its sweeper tentacles (thin, stinging filaments used to defend territory) are short and extend primarily at night. Even so, adequate spacing from all neighboring corals is recommended to accommodate colony growth over time.

Color variants: Pocillopora is available in green, pink, purple, yellow, and multicolored designer morphs — including named strains such as the 'Rainbow Pocillopora'. Some captive-bred designer morphs may perform well at slightly lower light and flow settings than wild-type specimens.

P. damicornis biology: Among known coral species, P. damicornis interacts with the widest diversity of Symbiodinium (zooxanthellae — the symbiotic algae that live within coral tissue and drive photosynthesis) types, with approximately 30 documented strains. This broad symbiotic flexibility may partly account for the species' relative environmental tolerance.
breedingNotes: Fragging — cutting the colony into smaller pieces — is the primary propagation method in captivity and is widely practiced. Colonies can be fragged with scissors or bone cutters, and pieces as small as about 1/2 inch typically encrust and attach to frag plugs or rubble within a few weeks. Sexual reproduction occurs via brooding: eggs and sperm are fertilized internally, and planula larvae (free-swimming coral juveniles) are released in batches timed near the new moon. Released larvae can remain viable for up to 100 days, which in the wild enables very wide geographic dispersal. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly attempted in the hobby.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Pink Cauliflower Pocillopora Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked all care parameters (temperature, pH, salinity, kH, lighting, flow, placement, care level, temperament, feeding). Confirmed identical values to the Green Cauliflower listing: 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, High lighting, Strong flow, Middle to Top placement, Moderate care, Peaceful temperament, micro-plankton twice weekly.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pocillopora damicornis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Pocilloporidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range (East Africa, Red Sea, Japan, Indonesia, Australia, Hawaii, Easter Island, western Central America), depth range (0–40 m, most common 5–20 m), colony size (up to 30 cm), reproduction (brooding hermaphrodite, larvae viable ~100 days), and bleaching resistance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pocillopora (genus)
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy and common name confirmation (cauliflower corals, brush corals). Confirmed family Pocilloporidae and feeding behavior (polyp tentacle extension primarily at night).

additional.name: World Wide Corals — WWC Rainbow Pocillopora Coral
additional.notes: WWC describes this captive-bred designer morph as requiring 'medium light' and 'medium flow' and as 'an excellent choice for beginners.' This is slightly more conservative than LiveAquaria's High/Strong recommendations for wild-type specimens, likely reflecting the captive-bred morph's positioning lower in the tank. The genus-level care parameters follow LiveAquaria, which covers standard wild-type Pocillopora. The beginner-friendliness characterization is consistent across sources.
